title: Beyond Hormuz: Iran’s Ten-Corridor Logistics Adaptation under Blockade Pressure
subtitle: Caspian Shipping, Central Asian Rail, and the Multi-Domain Logic of Threshold-Delaying Supply
title_normalized: beyond hormuz: iran’s ten-corridor logistics adaptation under blockade pressure

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

publisher: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC
doi: To be assigned after Crossref membership approval
description: This policy brief examines Iran’s reported ten-corridor logistics adaptation under blockade pressure. It analyzes how alternative land-sea corridors, Caspian shipping, Central Asian rail connectivity, air replenishment routes, and residual Hormuz/Persian Gulf movement combine into a multi-domain, threshold-delaying supply system.
abstract: This policy brief assesses Iran’s reported plan to develop ten distinct alternative trade corridors under blockade pressure. It argues that the plan should not be understood as a replacement for the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz logistics system, but as a distributed resilience strategy designed to preserve minimum viable flows. Because Iran has not released a full corridor-by-corridor list, the brief reconstructs a plausible ten-corridor framework based on public reporting, geography, trade patterns, and observed logistics behavior. It further shows that Iran’s adaptation extends beyond land and sea routes: air connectivity adds a high-value replenishment layer, while residual movement through a degraded but not fully sealed Hormuz/Persian Gulf window preserves limited maritime flow. The brief concludes that Iran’s ten-corridor adaptation does not end the blockade problem; it turns it into a long-duration contest of logistics friction.

figures.title: Regional System Health Index by Time
figures.description: Compares the time-varying SHI of the Caspian System and Persian Gulf System from April 10 to April 28, 2026, using Iran time.

figures.title: China–Central Asia Rail Activity, January–March 2025/2026 (TEU)
figures.description: Compares monthly outbound TEU totals for January–March 2025 and 2026.
figures.source: Author’s compilation based on China Railway Express data

figures.title: Persian Gulf System Monitor, April 9–26, 2026
figures.description: Compares estimated daily baseline output in the Persian Gulf system against a historical baseline.
figures.source: Author’s Persian Gulf System Monitor dataset

tables.title: Plausible Ten-Corridor Framework under Blockade Pressure
tables.description: Analytical reconstruction of ten possible alternative corridors, including route type, primary function, capacity, strategic effect, and confidence level.
tables.source: Author’s reconstruction based on public reporting, regional trade geography, author’s Caspian/Persian Gulf monitoring dataset, and China Railway Express data through March 2026

tables.title: Iran’s Multi-Domain Logistics Adaptation System
tables.description: Three-layer 10+2 analytical framework grouping Iran’s adaptation into ten land-sea alternative corridors, air replenishment routes, and residual Hormuz/Persian Gulf flow.
tables.source: Author’s analysis based on the reconstructed corridor framework in Table 1 and observed logistics indicators
